items.passage:
Prescribed burning is a land-management practice in which fires are deliberately set under controlled conditions to reduce flammable vegetation. For much of the twentieth century, managers pursued the opposite goal, treating every wildfire as a threat to be extinguished as quickly as possible. [■] This policy of total suppression allowed dead branches, fallen leaves, and dense undergrowth to pile up across forest floors year after year.

These accumulations proved dangerous. [■] Deprived of the small, frequent fires that had once cleared them naturally, many forests built up enormous fuel loads that fed rare but catastrophic blazes. Ecologists gradually recognized that fire had always been part of these ecosystems and that removing it entirely disrupted the cycles on which certain plants depended. [■]

This insight reshaped modern practice. Controlled burns are conducted in cool, damp seasons, when flames stay low and predictable. [■] Such burns thin the underbrush, return nutrients to the soil, and open bare ground where fire-adapted seeds can sprout. However, prescribed burning still carries risk, since shifting winds may push a planned fire beyond its intended boundaries, and smoke can draw complaints from nearby residents.
paragraphs: Prescribed burning is a land-management practice in which fires are deliberately set under controlled conditions to reduce flammable vegetation. For much of the twentieth century, managers pursued the opposite goal, treating every wildfire as a threat to be extinguished as quickly as possible. [■] This policy of total suppression allowed dead branches, fallen leaves, and dense undergrowth to pile up across forest floors year after year.

These accumulations proved dangerous. [■] Deprived of the small, frequent fires that had once cleared them naturally, many forests built up enormous fuel loads that fed rare but catastrophic blazes. Ecologists gradually recognized that fire had always been part of these ecosystems and that removing it entirely disrupted the cycles on which certain plants depended. [■]

This insight reshaped modern practice. Controlled burns are conducted in cool, damp seasons, when flames stay low and predictable. [■] Such burns thin the underbrush, return nutrients to the soil, and open bare ground where fire-adapted seeds can sprout. However, prescribed burning still carries risk, since shifting winds may push a planned fire beyond its intended boundaries, and smoke can draw complaints from nearby residents.

questions.stem: The word "catastrophic" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: frequent
options.B: predictable
options.C: disastrous
options.D: minor

questions.explanation: The passage contrasts "rare but catastrophic blazes" fed by heavy fuel loads with the small fires of the past; "catastrophic" here means extremely destructive, or disastrous.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what was the goal of fire managers for much of the twentieth century?

options.A: To extinguish every wildfire as rapidly as they could.
options.B: To set small controlled fires during the damp seasons.
options.C: To protect rare plant species from drifting smoke.
options.D: To clear forest land for timber harvesting and grazing.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that managers treated "every wildfire as a threat to be extinguished as quickly as possible," which option A paraphrases.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about forests before total suppression began?

options.A: They held more dead vegetation than forests do today.
options.B: They contained fewer plant species than modern forests.
options.C: They rarely burned because their soil stayed damp.
options.D: They burned in small, frequent fires that limited fuel buildup.

questions.explanation: The passage says forests were "deprived of the small, frequent fires that had once cleared them naturally," implying those fires previously kept fuel from accumulating.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention "the cycles on which certain plants depended" in paragraph 2?

options.A: To argue that some plant species are more valuable than others.
options.B: To illustrate that removing fire disrupted natural ecological processes.
options.C: To suggest that fire has almost no effect on plant life.
options.D: To describe the appearance of a typical forest floor.

questions.explanation: The clause supports the point that eliminating fire "disrupted the cycles" plants relied on, showing that suppression damaged ecological processes.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, some pine species have cones sealed shut by resin that only intense heat can melt, releasing their seeds only after a fire has passed.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: The first marked location [■]
options.B: The second marked location [■]
options.C: The third marked location [■]
options.D: The fourth marked location [■]

questions.explanation: The removed sentence gives a concrete example of plants that depend on fire, so it fits best at the third marker, right after "the cycles on which certain plants depended."

items.passage:
The placebo effect is a phenomenon in which a patient's symptoms improve after receiving a treatment that contains no active therapeutic ingredient. [■] This response poses a serious problem for medical research, because it can make an ineffective drug appear to work. [■] When patients expect relief, their reported pain, mood, or fatigue often shifts even though nothing chemically meaningful has entered their bodies.

To separate genuine drug effects from mere expectation, researchers developed the randomized controlled trial. Participants are divided by chance into two groups, one receiving the real medicine and the other an identical-looking placebo. [■] Because neither the patients nor the doctors know who received which, an arrangement called double-blinding, expectations are spread evenly across both groups.

This method has become the standard for testing new medicines. Any improvement seen in the placebo group is subtracted from the treatment group, leaving the drug's true effect. [■] However, the approach has limits, since some treatments are difficult to disguise, and the placebo response itself may vary from one culture to another, so results that hold in one country may not repeat elsewhere.
paragraphs: The placebo effect is a phenomenon in which a patient's symptoms improve after receiving a treatment that contains no active therapeutic ingredient. [■] This response poses a serious problem for medical research, because it can make an ineffective drug appear to work. [■] When patients expect relief, their reported pain, mood, or fatigue often shifts even though nothing chemically meaningful has entered their bodies.

To separate genuine drug effects from mere expectation, researchers developed the randomized controlled trial. Participants are divided by chance into two groups, one receiving the real medicine and the other an identical-looking placebo. [■] Because neither the patients nor the doctors know who received which, an arrangement called double-blinding, expectations are spread evenly across both groups.

This method has become the standard for testing new medicines. Any improvement seen in the placebo group is subtracted from the treatment group, leaving the drug's true effect. [■] However, the approach has limits, since some treatments are difficult to disguise, and the placebo response itself may vary from one culture to another, so results that hold in one country may not repeat elsewhere.

questions.stem: The word "disguise" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: conceal
options.B: cure
options.C: measure
options.D: worsen

questions.explanation: The passage notes that some treatments are hard to hide from participants, so "disguise" here means to conceal or hide the treatment's identity.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how are participants assigned to the two groups?

options.A: According to the severity of their reported symptoms.
options.B: Randomly, by chance.
options.C: Based on their previous response to medication.
options.D: According to the treating doctor's own judgment.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "participants are divided by chance into two groups," which option B restates.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Patients' expectations can influence their reported symptoms.
options.B: In double-blinding, doctors do not know who received the placebo.
options.C: The placebo response is identical across all cultures.
options.D: Improvement in the placebo group is subtracted from the treatment group.

questions.explanation: The passage says the placebo response "may vary from one culture to another," so the claim that it is identical across cultures is not supported.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about a drug tested without any placebo comparison?

options.A: It would show no improvement in patients at all.
options.B: It would be cheaper to develop than other drugs.
options.C: It must contain an active therapeutic ingredient.
options.D: Its apparent benefits could stem from expectation rather than chemistry.

questions.explanation: Because the placebo effect can make an ineffective drug "appear to work," a drug tested without a placebo control could show benefits caused by expectation rather than by the drug itself.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, a plain sugar pill described as a powerful painkiller can measurably ease a person's discomfort.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: The first marked location [■]
options.B: The second marked location [■]
options.C: The third marked location [■]
options.D: The fourth marked location [■]

questions.explanation: The removed sentence is a concrete example of the placebo effect just defined, so it fits best at the first marker, immediately after the definition of the effect.

items.passage:
A planetary atmosphere is the envelope of gases that a planet's gravity holds close to its surface. Mars presents a striking puzzle, because surface features that were carved by ancient rivers suggest that the planet once possessed a thick, warm atmosphere. Today its air is thin and cold, less than one percent as dense as Earth's. [■] Explaining how so substantial an envelope could nearly disappear became a central question in planetary science.

The leading explanation centers on the planet's magnetic field. Earth generates a strong global field that deflects the solar wind, a stream of charged particles flowing outward from the Sun. [■] Mars, by contrast, lost much of its internal heat early in its history, and its core solidified, shutting down the dynamo that had once sustained such a field. [■] Without this protective shield, the solar wind struck the upper atmosphere directly and stripped gas molecules into space over billions of years.

This model fits data from orbiting spacecraft, which still detect gas escaping from Mars today. [■] Yet the account remains incomplete, because researchers cannot fully reconstruct how quickly the loss occurred, and some of the missing gas may instead be locked within minerals beneath the surface.
paragraphs: A planetary atmosphere is the envelope of gases that a planet's gravity holds close to its surface. Mars presents a striking puzzle, because surface features that were carved by ancient rivers suggest that the planet once possessed a thick, warm atmosphere. Today its air is thin and cold, less than one percent as dense as Earth's. [■] Explaining how so substantial an envelope could nearly disappear became a central question in planetary science.

The leading explanation centers on the planet's magnetic field. Earth generates a strong global field that deflects the solar wind, a stream of charged particles flowing outward from the Sun. [■] Mars, by contrast, lost much of its internal heat early in its history, and its core solidified, shutting down the dynamo that had once sustained such a field. [■] Without this protective shield, the solar wind struck the upper atmosphere directly and stripped gas molecules into space over billions of years.

This model fits data from orbiting spacecraft, which still detect gas escaping from Mars today. [■] Yet the account remains incomplete, because researchers cannot fully reconstruct how quickly the loss occurred, and some of the missing gas may instead be locked within minerals beneath the surface.

questions.stem: The word "deflects" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: attracts
options.B: absorbs
options.C: repels
options.D: creates

questions.explanation: The field turns the solar wind away from Earth so it does not reach the atmosphere; "deflects" therefore means repels or pushes aside.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happened to Mars's magnetic field?

options.A: It grew stronger as the planet lost its internal heat.
options.B: It shut down after the planet's core cooled and solidified.
options.C: It was destroyed by a large impact early in the planet's history.
options.D: It reversed direction, weakening its protective effect on the air.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says Mars's core solidified, "shutting down the dynamo that had once sustained such a field," meaning the field ceased once the core cooled.

questions.stem: The phrase "this protective shield" in paragraph 2 refers to

options.A: a global magnetic field
options.B: the solar wind
options.C: the planet's molten core
options.D: the upper atmosphere

questions.explanation: The paragraph describes the magnetic field as deflecting the solar wind; "this protective shield" refers back to that global magnetic field, which Mars had lost.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from the passage about Earth's atmosphere?

options.A: Earth's magnetic field helps shield its atmosphere from the solar wind.
options.B: Earth is losing its atmosphere faster than Mars once did.
options.C: Earth's atmosphere is made of the same gases as Mars's air.
options.D: Earth's core has already solidified and stopped its dynamo.

questions.explanation: Because Earth's field "deflects the solar wind" while Mars lost its field and its air, it follows that Earth's magnetic field helps protect Earth's atmosphere from stripping.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, the MAVEN spacecraft has measured ions being carried away from the planet by gusts in the solar wind.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: The first marked location [■]
options.B: The second marked location [■]
options.C: The third marked location [■]
options.D: The fourth marked location [■]

questions.explanation: The removed sentence gives a concrete example of spacecraft detecting escaping gas, so it fits best at the fourth marker, just after the mention of orbiting spacecraft that detect gas escaping today.

items.passage:
Corrosion is the gradual deterioration of a metal caused by chemical reactions with substances in its surroundings. The most familiar example is rust, which forms when iron is exposed to oxygen and moisture over time. Early builders noticed that iron tools, bridges, and ships weakened and flaked as reddish-brown rust spread across their surfaces, yet they lacked any clear understanding of why the damage occurred.

This limited knowledge gradually gave way to practical remedies. Engineers learned to slow corrosion by coating iron with paint or grease, which blocked contact between the metal and the surrounding air. A more durable method, galvanization, covered steel with a thin layer of zinc that corroded in place of the iron beneath it. Such barriers extended the life of structures, though they often wore away over time and needed renewal.

This search for protection culminated in materials that resist corrosion from within. In the early twentieth century, metallurgists developed stainless steel by adding chromium, which forms an invisible, self-repairing oxide film on the surface. Cathodic protection, in which a more reactive metal is deliberately sacrificed, now guards pipelines and ship hulls. However, no method stops corrosion entirely, and metal structures still demand constant inspection and care.
paragraphs: Corrosion is the gradual deterioration of a metal caused by chemical reactions with substances in its surroundings. The most familiar example is rust, which forms when iron is exposed to oxygen and moisture over time. Early builders noticed that iron tools, bridges, and ships weakened and flaked as reddish-brown rust spread across their surfaces, yet they lacked any clear understanding of why the damage occurred.

This limited knowledge gradually gave way to practical remedies. Engineers learned to slow corrosion by coating iron with paint or grease, which blocked contact between the metal and the surrounding air. A more durable method, galvanization, covered steel with a thin layer of zinc that corroded in place of the iron beneath it. Such barriers extended the life of structures, though they often wore away over time and needed renewal.

This search for protection culminated in materials that resist corrosion from within. In the early twentieth century, metallurgists developed stainless steel by adding chromium, which forms an invisible, self-repairing oxide film on the surface. Cathodic protection, in which a more reactive metal is deliberately sacrificed, now guards pipelines and ship hulls. However, no method stops corrosion entirely, and metal structures still demand constant inspection and care.

questions.stem: The word "durable" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: expensive
options.B: attractive
options.C: modern
options.D: long-lasting

questions.explanation: Galvanization is described as extending the life of structures better than paint, so "durable" means able to last a long time.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how does galvanization protect steel?

options.A: A layer of paint reacts chemically with the zinc coating.
options.B: A zinc coating corrodes in place of the iron underneath.
options.C: Chromium is added to strengthen the steel's surface.
options.D: The steel is heated until its outer layer is sealed.

questions.explanation: The passage says galvanization uses "a thin layer of zinc that corroded in place of the iron beneath it," which option B restates.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about early builders?

options.A: They already knew how to prevent iron from rusting.
options.B: They could observe rust damage without understanding its cause.
options.C: They avoided using iron in their largest structures.
options.D: They believed that rust made iron tools stronger.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that builders noticed the damage "yet they lacked any clear understanding of why the damage occurred," implying they saw the effect but not the cause.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention paint and grease in paragraph 2?

options.A: To argue that these methods worked better than galvanization did.
options.B: To show that surface coatings had no effect on corrosion.
options.C: To illustrate early barrier methods that kept air away from the metal.
options.D: To describe the changing appearance of corroded iron tools.

questions.explanation: Paint and grease are given as examples of coatings that "blocked contact between the metal and the surrounding air," illustrating early barrier methods against corrosion.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Stainless steel resists corrosion by forming a layer of zinc.
options.B: Rust forms when iron is exposed to oxygen and moisture.
options.C: Cathodic protection sacrifices a more reactive metal.
options.D: Paint slows corrosion by blocking contact between metal and air.

questions.explanation: The passage says stainless steel resists corrosion through a chromium oxide film, not zinc; the zinc layer belongs to galvanization, so option A is not supported.

items.passage:
The Silk Road was a network of overland routes that connected China with central Asia, India, and the Mediterranean world. Moving goods along it posed a formidable challenge, for the journey stretched thousands of kilometers across deserts, mountains, and lands governed by rival powers. No single trader could survive the entire distance, and the scarcity of water together with the constant threat of banditry made long stretches nearly impassable.

Merchants overcame these obstacles through a system of relays rather than continuous travel. Goods changed hands repeatedly, passing from one regional trader to the next, so that a bolt of silk was often traded through a dozen markets before reaching the West. Oasis towns grew into vital waystations where caravans rested and exchanged animals, and local rulers protected the routes in return for taxes on the passing trade.

This relay system carried far more than commodities. Religions, artistic styles, and technologies traveled the same paths and reshaped the societies they touched, while deadly diseases also spread along the crowded routes. The network gradually declined as sea routes proved cheaper and safer, though its long influence on cultural exchange endured. However, historians caution that the term 'Silk Road' oversimplifies a shifting web of connections that never followed a single fixed path.
paragraphs: The Silk Road was a network of overland routes that connected China with central Asia, India, and the Mediterranean world. Moving goods along it posed a formidable challenge, for the journey stretched thousands of kilometers across deserts, mountains, and lands governed by rival powers. No single trader could survive the entire distance, and the scarcity of water together with the constant threat of banditry made long stretches nearly impassable.

Merchants overcame these obstacles through a system of relays rather than continuous travel. Goods changed hands repeatedly, passing from one regional trader to the next, so that a bolt of silk was often traded through a dozen markets before reaching the West. Oasis towns grew into vital waystations where caravans rested and exchanged animals, and local rulers protected the routes in return for taxes on the passing trade.

This relay system carried far more than commodities. Religions, artistic styles, and technologies traveled the same paths and reshaped the societies they touched, while deadly diseases also spread along the crowded routes. The network gradually declined as sea routes proved cheaper and safer, though its long influence on cultural exchange endured. However, historians caution that the term 'Silk Road' oversimplifies a shifting web of connections that never followed a single fixed path.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: How traders managed the challenges of the Silk Road and the network's wider impact.
options.B: The role that oasis towns played as resting places for passing caravans.
options.C: The kinds of diseases that spread between Asia and Europe over the routes.
options.D: The reasons that sea routes eventually replaced overland trade with China.

questions.explanation: The passage moves from the difficulties of the route, to how merchants overcame them with relays, to the network's broad cultural effects, which option A captures in full scope.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why could no single trader complete the entire route?

options.A: Because rival powers blocked foreign merchants from crossing their lands.
options.B: Because the trade goods were far too heavy to carry that distance.
options.C: Because the vast distance and constant dangers made the trip impassable.
options.D: Because traders had no accurate maps of the deserts and mountains.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 attributes the difficulty to the journey stretching thousands of kilometers with scarce water and banditry that "made long stretches nearly impassable."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It provides historical background for the events described in paragraph 1.
options.B: It offers a counterexample that contradicts paragraph 1's main claim.
options.C: It restates paragraph 1's central point using more technical language.
options.D: It describes how merchants solved the problem raised in paragraph 1.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 presents the problem of the dangerous, impassable route, and paragraph 2 explains the relay system by which merchants "overcame these obstacles," a problem-solution relationship.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about the effects of the Silk Road?

options.A: It had little lasting effect once trade shifted to the sea.
options.B: It caused rival powers to form lasting military alliances.
options.C: Its impact reached beyond commerce into culture and daily life.
options.D: It spread diseases only after sea routes had replaced it.

questions.explanation: Because religions, artistic styles, and technologies "reshaped the societies they touched," it can be inferred that the network's impact extended well beyond the trade in goods.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, why did the Silk Road decline?

options.A: Because oasis towns stopped protecting the passing caravans.
options.B: Because sea routes became cheaper and safer than overland travel.
options.C: Because demand for silk in the West suddenly collapsed.
options.D: Because rival powers closed their borders to all trade.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states that "the network gradually declined as sea routes proved cheaper and safer," which option B paraphrases.
